title: Treasurer waves off economists’ warnings
full_text: Other Editions 01 May 2026 TheNation Treasurer waves off economists’ warnings The Australian Thomas Henry 30 April 2026 515 words English AUSTLN 4 © News Pty Limited. No redistribution is permitted. Jim Chalmers has declared the Australian economy is “better placed” than other countries to weather economic shocks stemming from war in the Middle East, having outstripped the growth of most G20 nations in the lead-up to the conflict. After the first full inflation reading since the outbreak of war in Iran showed prices surging by 4.6 per cent in the year to March, the Treasurer claimed strong economic growth and low unemployment meant Australia was better prepared than other advanced economies. “We know the Australian economy is not immune from all of this global uncertainty and volatility and unpredictable conditions. But we are better placed and better prepared and with faster growth than any major advanced economy to finish out 2025,” Dr Chalmers told reporters in Brisbane. “We’ve got low unemployment, we’ve got solid wages growth. And so we’ve got pretty good foundations as we confront this period of heightened uncertainty in the global economy.” Australia’s annual economic growth of 2.6 per cent recorded in the final quarter of last year was beaten only by India, Indonesia, Saudi Arabia, China and Turkey according to the most recent OECD data. Despite the nation’s GDP growth outstripping the likes of the US, France, the UK, Italy, Brazil and Germany, economists have long held concerns around Australia’s resurgent inflation. Before the outbreak of war in the Middle East, inflation was already sitting well outside the Reserve Bank’s target band and Australia’s interest rate path was out of step with the majority of other advanced economies. Earlier this week, HSBC chief economist Paul Bloxham claimed that Australia was “one of the least well placed developed economies” to weather the global economic shock emanating from the Middle East. “For the RBA, the challenge is that inflation was already too high, even before the Middle East conflict energy price shock,” Mr Bloxham said. “At the same time, the Australian economy has also been operating beyond its sustainable capacity and the jobs market has been tight. In our view, this combination makes Australia one of the least well-placed developed economies to deal with the inflation shock that is arriving.” Deloitte’s Stephen Smith on Wednesday claimed Australia’s inflationary starting point heading into the global oil shock meant the RBA was left with little choice but to unwind last year’s rate cuts. “Today’s CPI print, the first to partially reflect the Strait of Hormuz closure, points to a rate hike from the Reserve Bank next week. That rate hike is not guaranteed, but Australia’s starting point for inflation heading into this crisis likely leaves the central bank with little choice,” he said. “The Australian economy was already operating at its speed limit at the start of the year.” After the release of the March inflation reading, financial markets had priced in more than a 70 per cent chance of another 0.25 percentage point rate hike at the RBA’s meeting next week. summary: Treasurer Jim Chalmers asserts that Australia is better positioned than other advanced economies to handle economic shocks from the ongoing Middle East conflict, citing strong growth and low unemployment. However, economists express concerns about Australia's high inflation and its implications for monetary policy, suggesting that the Reserve Bank may need to raise interest rates. The latest inflation data indicates a significant rise, increasing the likelihood of a rate hike in the near future.

key_points: Australia's economy is reportedly better placed to withstand global shocks due to strong growth and low unemployment.
Concerns persist regarding high inflation, which is already above the Reserve Bank's target band.
Financial markets are anticipating a potential interest rate hike following the latest inflation readings.
relevance: Understanding the economic landscape and inflation trends is crucial for actuaries and financial services professionals in assessing risks and making informed decisions.
